elements.sent-2411-001: Deciding bank interest rates has been a long-standing challenge in finance.
elements.sent-2411-002: It is crucial to ensure that the selected rates balance market share and profitability.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, traditional approaches typically focus on the interest rate changes of individual banks, often neglecting the interactions with other banks in the market.
elements.sent-2411-004: This work proposes a novel framework that models the interest rate problem as a major-minor mean field game within the context of an interbank game.
elements.sent-2411-005: To incorporate the complex interactions between banks, we utilize mean-field theory and employ impulsive control to model the overhead in rate adjustments.
elements.sent-2411-006: Ultimately, we solve this optimal control problem using a new deep Q-network method, which iterates the parameterized action value functions for major and minor players and updates the networks in a Fictitious Play way.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our proposed algorithm converges, offering a solution that enables the analysis of strategies for major and minor players in the market under the Nash Equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-008: Effective competition among banks is an important safeguard for maintaining stability in the banking and financial sector \cite{boyd2005theory .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Since the establishment of Bertrand competition model, bank can determine their share in the market by setting the number and price of financial products.
elements.sent-2411-010: Therefore, attracting more customers to bring in more revenue without sacrificing profitability by rationalising the interest rates on savings accounts has become the focus of attention in the inter-bank game \cite{van2013impact , which leads to an optimal control problem.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Most of the existing interest rate models focus only on the process of interest rate change of a single bank or institution.
elements.sent-2411-012: For example, the Vasicek model \cite{mamon2004three is a short-rate model that describes the evolution of interest rates driven by only market risk.}
elements.sent-2411-013: The Hull-White model \cite{hull1996using is a more general formulation that parameterise the evolution of interest rate with respect to time.}
elements.sent-2411-014: However, although being frequently referred to by practitioners, none of the previous literature has addressed the problem from a multi-agent aspect, where multiple competitor banks aim to optimize the interest rate they offer and maximize profits over their market share.
elements.sent-2411-015: In this work, we propose a novel framework that captures the interactions in the interbank game.
elements.sent-2411-016: This is the first framework that incorporates major-minor play theories to close the gap between well-known big banks and ordinary financial institutions (or the digital banks in the post-millennium era).
elements.sent-2411-017: We then further exploit the mean field wheel to summarise the complex relationship of competition among banks.
elements.sent-2411-018: An impulsive control was also added to better incorporate the process of interest rate change into the proposed framework.
elements.sent-2411-019: Based on the above framework, we specify the definition of the Nash equilibrium that guides the control strategies for the interbank game.
elements.sent-2411-020: Ultimately, we solve the major-minor mean field game problem using the deep Q-network method.
elements.sent-2411-021: This novel approach numerically addresses major-minor mean field games, which are typically more complex due to the presence of common noise from the major player and the stochastic mean field measure flow.
elements.sent-2411-022: Moreover, our method applies to cases of continuous state space.
elements.sent-2411-023: To the best of our knowledge, there are no existing algorithms for this type of problem.
elements.sent-2411-024: The most relevant one is the algorithm proposed by Cui \cite{cui2024learning , which is designed for finite state spaces.}
elements.sent-2411-025: In contrast, our neural network-based approach can handle the general case and provide robust solutions.
elements.sent-2411-026: The contributions of this work are summarised below.
elements.sent-2411-027: This is the first work to model the bank interest-rate problem as an interbank game.
elements.sent-2411-028: We establish a major-minor mean field game framework, mimicking the market role heterogeneity and incorporating impulsive controls to account for the adjustment costs in interest rate changes.
elements.sent-2411-029: We design a deep Q-network algorithm to solve the major-minor mean field game, allowing for continuous or large state and action spaces and overcoming the challenges of averaging neural networks inherent in Fictitious Play-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-030: The algorithm converges, providing robust solutions that present the strategies for major and minor players under Nash Equilibrium, supported by experimental results in Section \ref{sec:exp .}
elements.sent-2411-031: The theory of Mean-Field Game (MFG) theory, introduced and developed by Lasry and Lions \cite{lasry2007mean and Huang et al. \cite{ huang2006large , incorporated the mean-field methodology widely used in fields of physics and chemistry into controlled games.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: In the regime of mean-field games, all atomic players are supposed to be homogeneous and symmetric and interact with each other.
elements.sent-2411-033: The number of players is assumed to be infinity, hence the interaction with other players can be characterized by the interaction with the distribution of players \cite{carmona2018probabilistic .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Recently, it has become a promising approach for modelling games with a large scale of interacting players \cite{tembine2020covid .}
elements.sent-2411-035: In finance and economics, extensive literature can be found for modelling market dynamics and systemic risks \cite{carmona2020applications , the construction of new price-impact models \cite{carmona2015probabilistic , the growth model in macro-economics \cite{achdou2022income , and the energy production and management \cite{elie2021mean .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: The pivotal objective of most research is the Nash Equilibrium, which is when each player in the game approaches its optimality against other players.
elements.sent-2411-037: For example, the existence analysis can be shown based on the contraction in fixed-point iterations \cite{carmona2018probabilistic .}
elements.sent-2411-038: We refer the reader to \cite{lauriere2022learning for a more comprehensive review of applications and theoretical analysis for mean-field games in different settings.}
elements.subsection-2411-003: Major and Minor Players in MFG
elements.sent-2411-039: The major player was first introduced into a linear-quadratic infinite-horizon model by Huang \cite{huang2010large .}
elements.sent-2411-040: Distinguished with the assumption in classical MFG that each individual is symmetric and asymptotically negligible, there exist players who have a strong influence on the system.
elements.sent-2411-041: The major-minor MFG is surely an important case in practice.
elements.sent-2411-042: Pointed out as an example in \cite{chen2023risk , in the price decision-making process, the decisions of innumerable minor companies are always affected by some major companies.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Similar ideas are explored in analyzing interactions between large and small financial institutions by Bensoussan et al.\cite{bensoussan2016mean and Carmona et al.\cite{carmona2014probabilistic , and more applications can be found in \cite{dayanikli2023multi, carmona2022mean .}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: The major-minor MFG is considered a generalization of the common noise problem (see \cite{carmona2018probabilistic for a basic framework).}
elements.sent-2411-045: Since the common noise from the major player influences all minor players, the mean-field flow becomes stochastic, complicating both theoretical analysis and empirical implementation.
elements.sent-2411-046: The theory of major-minor MFG was further developed in the Linear-Quadratic-Gaussian (LQG) model \cite{firoozi2022lqg .}
elements.sent-2411-047: Other works proposed general frameworks under different settings, including formulating the Nash certainty equivalence principle \cite{huang2020linear , the stochastic Hamilton Jacobian Bellman \cite{bensoussan2016mean , and master equations \cite{cardaliaguet2020remarks .}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Another line of research focuses on the Stackelberg Equilibrium \cite{guo2022optimization , in contrast with the Nash Equilibrium of all players.}
elements.sent-2411-049: It is different from our settings, as in the Stackelberg Equilibrium, the major players (leaders) decide first and minor players (followers) respond to these decisions.
elements.sent-2411-050: In this paper, we will focus on the computation of the Nash Equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-051: Our approach is based on Reinforcement Learning (RL), a prevailing way with numerous applications (see survey \cite{wang2022deep ).}
elements.sent-2411-052: For classical MFG, the basic methodology is alternating the estimation of population distribution and policy updates.
elements.sent-2411-053: A simple approach is to update the policy by the best response against other players \cite{guo2019learning .}
elements.sent-2411-054: There are several methods to stabilize the convergence process: the fictitious Play averages the past distributions or policies \cite{perrin2020fictitious ; the online mirror descent and its variants accumulate the past Q-function \cite{perolat2021scaling ; and other methods based on regularization ensure convergence, albeit with a bias \cite{cui2021approximately .}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: When the state and action spaces are continuous, a tabular method will be infeasible and we should resort to the help of neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-056: However, the network parameterization of the policy or the Q-function faces the difficulty of taking averages, as emphasized in \cite{lauriere2022scalable .}
elements.sent-2411-057: It is notable that in \cite{lauriere2022scalable , the authors proposed the Munchausen Online Mirror Descent to overcome such difficulty, following the idea of Munchausen reparameterization \cite{vieillard2020munchausen .}}
elements.sent-2411-058: In contrast, our proposed approach adopts a novel average technique by recognizing shallow neural networks as a function of measures as studied in \cite{chen2023entropic , and our approach does not smooth the policy during iteration.}
elements.sent-2411-059: As for the Major-Minor player MFG, the most relevant paper to ours, Cui et al. designed a learning framework in discrete-time \cite{cui2024learning .}
elements.sent-2411-060: Fictitious play and projected mean-field algorithm were explored in \cite{cui2024learning .}
elements.sent-2411-061: Our Deep Q-Network (DQN) algorithm (follow the name in \cite{mnih2013playing ) is inspired by the thought of projection, nevertheless it is distinguished essentially from the one proposed in \cite{cui2024learning : the average is upon the Q-function; it includes the learning procedure; the average of network outputs utilizes a measure perspective.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: Impulse controls are mathematically formulated to characterize the impulsive behaviours that widely exist in biology, medicine and economics.
elements.sent-2411-063: In impulse control problems, the agent can determine a sequence of controlled jumps to optimize its return.
elements.sent-2411-064: The impulse control problem has sparked considerable interest in applications, especially in resource economics \cite{alvarez2001stochastic , portfolio optimization with transaction costs \cite{morton1995optimal , inventory control and dividend control \cite{bai2010optimal , and the modelling of exchange and interest rates \cite{mundaca1998optimal .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: Theoretically, the impulse control problems are naturally connected to the viscosity solution of Quasi-Variational Inequalities \cite{reisinger2020error .}
elements.sent-2411-066: Games of impulse control type are studied in \cite{azimzadeh2019zero,aid2020nonzero under various settings.}
elements.sent-2411-067: We refer the readers to \cite{oksendal2019stochastic for a comprehensive discussion and formulation of the impulse control problem.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Denote the time space as $\mathcal T$ and consider the continuous-time setting $\mathcal T = \mathbb R^+$.
elements.sent-2411-069: In the market, there are $M$ major banks, with each bank holding a proportion $(p_t^{(i) )_{i=1 ^M$ of the entire market at time $t\in\mathcal T$.}}
elements.sent-2411-070: Additionally, there are $N$ minor banks holding proportions $(p_t^{(i) )_{i=M+1 ^{M+N}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-071: These proportions satisfy the condition $\sum_{i=1 ^{M+N p_t^{(i) = 1$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: In the context of the mean-field game setting, the number of minor banks $N$ tends to infinity, and we assume that all minor banks are homogeneous while the major banks are not.
elements.sent-2411-073: Each bank will control its deposit interest rate $(r_t^i)_{i=1 ^{M+N}$, which will influence its respective market proportion.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Let $(\kappa^{(i) )_{i=1 ^{M+N}$ and $(\delta^{(i) )_{i=1 ^{M+N}$ denote the escape rates and viscosity of clients, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-075: The dynamics of proportion change are characterized by: \begin{equation}\label{eq:density_transform} \begin{aligned} \frac{dp_t^{(i)}}{dt} = \sum_{i'\neq i}\kappa^{(i')} (r_t^{(i)}-r_t^{(i')}-\delta^{(i')})^+p_t^{(i)} -{\sum_{i'\neq i} \kappa^{(i)}(r_t^{(i)}-r_t^{(i')}+\delta^{(i)})^-{p_t^{(i)}}}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} The viscosity parameters $(\delta^{(i) )_{i=1 ^{M+N}$ can be understood as thresholds: once bank $i$ offers a higher rate than bank $i'$ to the extent that $r_t^{(i) -r_t^{(i') -\delta^{(i') >0$, the clients of bank $i'$ changes their minds, causing the proportion to move from bank $i'$ to $i$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: It can also be observed that the larger the $\kappa^{i'}$ is, the more inclined the clients of bank $i'$ are to switch.
elements.sent-2411-077: Furthermore, from \eqref{eq:density_transform , it follows that the sum of all proportions remains constant over time, i.e., $\sum_{i=1 ^{M+N p_t^{(i) = 1$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: Banks' adjustments to deposit interest rates take the form of impulsive controls.. Specifically, \begin{equation}r_t^{(i)} = \sum_j \mathbf{1}_{\{t\ge\tau_j^{(i)}\}} \theta_j^{(i)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-079: In other words, each bank adjusts its interest rate at stopping times, together with deciding the most beneficial change magnitudes.
elements.sent-2411-080: Assume the total volume of bank deposits is fixed at $W$.
elements.sent-2411-081: At time $t$, the Profit \& Losses (P\&Ls) of bank $i$ from deposits are modeled by $Wp_t^{(i) (l_t^{(i) +r_t^c-r_t^{(i) )$, where $(l^i)_{i=1 ^{M+N}$ represents the liquidity premium, $r_t^c$ denotes the Central Bank interest rate.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-082: Consequently, each bank faces a trade-off in setting its interest rate: it must avoid setting the rate too low, which would result in a loss of customers, or too high, which would result in excessive interest payments to depositors.
elements.sent-2411-083: The total discounted expected P\&L of the bank is given by: { \begin{equation}J^{(i)} = \mathbb E\Bigg[\int_{\mathcal T} We^{-k^{(i)}t}p_t^{(i)}(l_t^{(i)}+r_t^c-r_t^{(i)}) dt - \sum_j \mathbf{1}_{\{\tau_j^{(i)}\in\mathcal T\}} C^{(i)}(\theta_j^{(i)})\Bigg],\end{equation} where $C^{(i) (\theta_j^{(i) )>0$ represents the cost incurred by banks in adjusting interest rates, and $e^{k^{(i) t}$ is the discounting factor.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: As a result, banks cannot make adjustments continuously or without careful consideration.
elements.sent-2411-085: In our model, the Central Bank rate is described by the jump process \begin{equation}\label{eq:CB_rate_dynamic} dr_t^c = \int_{\mathbb{R}} \alpha(t, r_{t-}^c, z) N(dt, dz),\end{equation} where $ N(dt, dz) $ is the Poisson random measure (refer to \cite{oksendal2019stochastic for its definition).}
elements.sent-2411-086: The integral variable $z$ represents the size of a jump that happens in the Poisson process, $ \alpha(t, r_{t- ^c, z) $ characterizes the magnitude of these jumps, and $ r_{t- $ denotes the left limit of the rate process.}}
elements.sent-2411-087: The randomness of the central bank rate serves as an external source of noise, affecting all players simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-088: In numerical experiments, we discrete the time space and treat the central bank rate dynamics as random transitions between states.
elements.sent-2411-089: All minor banks are assumed to be homogeneous, with parameters $\delta^{(i) = \delta$, $\kappa^{(i) =\kappa$ for $i=M+1,...,M+N$.}}
elements.sent-2411-090: We assume there are infinitely many minor banks.
elements.sent-2411-091: The proportion change dynamics in \eqref{eq:density_transform are modified to \begin{equation}\label{eq:density_transform_modified} { \begin{aligned} \frac{d p_t^{(i)}}{dt} = \sum_{i'=1}^M\kappa^{(i')}(r_t^{(i)}-r_t^{(i')}-\delta^{(i')})^+ p_t^{(i')} \quad + \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i'=M+1}^{M+N}\kappa(r_t^{(i)}-r_t-\delta)^+ p_t -{\sum_{i'=1}^M \kappa^{(i)}(r_t^{(i)}-r_t^{(i')}+\delta^{(i)})^-{ p_t^{(i)}}} \quad - \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i'=M+1}^{M+N} \kappa(r_t^{(i)}-r_t+\delta^{(i)})^- p_t^{(i)}, \end{aligned} }\end{equation} where we set a multiplier $1/N$ to rescale the proportion exchanges between each pair of major and minor banks.}
elements.sent-2411-092: Rescaling the proportion of minor banks as $\bar p_t^{(i) = Np_t^{(i)}$ for $i=M+1,...,M+N$ and abusing the notation $\bar p_t^{(i) = p_t^{(i)}$ for $i=1,...,M$, we are ready to rewrite \eqref{eq:density_transform_modified as \begin{equation}\label{eq:density_transform_modified_integral_major} { \begin{aligned} \frac{d\bar p_t^{(i)}}{dt} = \sum_{i'=1}^M\kappa^{(i')}(r_t^{(i)}-r_t^{(i')}-\delta^{(i')})^+\bar p_t^{(i')} \quad + \int \kappa(r_t^{(i)}-r_t-\delta)^+\bar p_t\mu_t^N(d\bar p_t,dr_t) -{\sum_{i'=1}^M \kappa^{(i)}(r_t^{(i)}-r_t^{(i')}+\delta^{(i)})^-{\bar p_t^{(i)}}} \quad -\int \kappa(r_t^{(i)}-r_t+\delta^{(i)})^-\bar p_t^{(i)}\mu_t^N(d\bar p_t,dr_t), \end{aligned} }\end{equation} for all banks $i=1,2,...,M+N$, where $\mu_t^N = \sum_{i=M+1 ^{M+N \delta_{(\bar p_t^{(i) ,r_t^{(i) )}$.}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}\label{eq:density_transform_modified_integral_major} {  \frac{d\bar p_t^{(i)}}{dt} = \sum_{i'=1}^M\kappa^{(i')}(r_t^{(i)}-r_t^{(i')}-\delta^{(i')})^+\bar p_t^{(i')} \quad + \int \kappa(r_t^{(i)}-r_t-\delta)^+\bar p_t\mu_t^N(d\bar p_t,dr_t) -{\sum_{i'=1}^M \kappa^{(i)}(r_t^{(i)}-r_t^{(i')}+\delta^{(i)})^-{\bar p_t^{(i)}}} \quad -\int \kappa(r_t^{(i)}-r_t+\delta^{(i)})^-\bar p_t^{(i)}\mu_t^N(d\bar p_t,dr_t),  }\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-093: The rescaling of dynamics and proportions ensures a proper definition of the distribution $\mu_t^N$ as $N$ becomes large.
elements.sent-2411-094: Under the mean-field framework, the dynamics of representative minor players are given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:density_transform_modified_integral_minor_rep} {\ \begin{aligned} \frac{d\bar p_t}{dt} = \sum_{i'=1}^M\kappa^{(i')}(r_t^{(i)}-r_t^{(i')}-\delta^{(i')})^+\bar p_t^{(i')} \quad + \int \kappa(r_t^{(i)}-r_t-\delta)^+ p_t\mu_t(d p_t,dr_t) -{\sum_{i'=1}^M \kappa^{(i)}(r_t^{(i)}-r_t^{(i')}+\delta)^-{\bar p_t}} \quad -\int \kappa(r_t^{(i)}-r_t+\delta)^-\bar p_t\mu_t(d p_t,dr_t), \eqcolon ~~b(\mathbf{\bar p_t^M},\mathbf{r_t^M},\bar p_t,r_t,\mu_t), \end{aligned} }\end{equation} where $\mathbf{\bar p_t^M}$ is the collection of all major players' proportions, and similarly, $\mathbf{r_t^M}$ is the collection of their interest rates.
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}\label{eq:density_transform_modified_integral_minor_rep} {\  \frac{d\bar p_t}{dt} = \sum_{i'=1}^M\kappa^{(i')}(r_t^{(i)}-r_t^{(i')}-\delta^{(i')})^+\bar p_t^{(i')} \quad + \int \kappa(r_t^{(i)}-r_t-\delta)^+ p_t\mu_t(d p_t,dr_t) -{\sum_{i'=1}^M \kappa^{(i)}(r_t^{(i)}-r_t^{(i')}+\delta)^-{\bar p_t}} \quad -\int \kappa(r_t^{(i)}-r_t+\delta)^-\bar p_t\mu_t(d p_t,dr_t), \eqcolon ~~b(\bar p_t^M,r_t^M,\bar p_t,r_t,\mu_t),  }\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-095: Here, $\bar p_t$ is regarded as a stochastic process with mean field flow $\mu_t = \text{Law (\bar p_t,r_t)$ at each time $t$.}
elements.sent-2411-096: On the other hand, the dynamics for the major players can be expressed as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:density_transform_modified_integral_major_MF} { \begin{aligned} \frac{d\bar p_t^{(i)}}{dt} = \sum_{i'=1}^M\kappa^{(i')}(r_t^{(i)}-r_t^{(i')}-\delta^{(i')})^+\bar p_t^{(i')} \quad + \int \kappa(r_t^{(i)}-r_t-\delta)^+\bar p_t\mu_t(d\bar p_t,dr_t) -{\sum_{i'=1}^M \kappa^{(i)}(r_t^{(i)}-r_t^{(i')}+\delta^{(i)})^-{\bar p_t^{(i)}}} \quad -\int \kappa(r_t^{(i)}-r_t+\delta^{(i)})^-\bar p_t^{(i)}\mu_t(d\bar p_t,dr_t), \eqcolon ~~b^{(i)}(\mathbf{\bar p_t^M},\mathbf{r_t^M},\mu_t) \end{aligned} }\end{equation} and again it satisfies that $\sum_{i=1 ^M \bar p_t^{(i) + \int \bar p_t \mu_t(d\bar p_t,dr_t) = 1$ for all $t\in\mathcal T$.}}
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}\label{eq:density_transform_modified_integral_major_MF} {  \frac{d\bar p_t^{(i)}}{dt} = \sum_{i'=1}^M\kappa^{(i')}(r_t^{(i)}-r_t^{(i')}-\delta^{(i')})^+\bar p_t^{(i')} \quad + \int \kappa(r_t^{(i)}-r_t-\delta)^+\bar p_t\mu_t(d\bar p_t,dr_t) -{\sum_{i'=1}^M \kappa^{(i)}(r_t^{(i)}-r_t^{(i')}+\delta^{(i)})^-{\bar p_t^{(i)}}} \quad -\int \kappa(r_t^{(i)}-r_t+\delta^{(i)})^-\bar p_t^{(i)}\mu_t(d\bar p_t,dr_t), \eqcolon ~~b^{(i)}(\bar p_t^M,r_t^M,\mu_t)  }\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-097: Consequently, the objective functions of the banks take the form \begin{equation}{ \begin{aligned} J^{(i)} (\mathbf{\tau}^{(i)},\mathbf \theta^{(i)}) := \mathbb E\Bigg[\int_{\mathcal T} We^{-k^{(i)}t}p_t^{(i)}(l_t^{(i)}+r_t^c-r_t^{(i)}) dt - \sum_j \mathbf{1}_{\{\tau_j^{(i)}\in\mathcal T\}} C^{(i)}(\theta_j^{(i)})\Bigg], J(\mathbf \tau,\mathbf \theta) :=\mathbb E\Bigg[\int_{\mathcal T} We^{-kt}\bar p_t(l_t+r_t^c-r_t) dt - \sum_j \mathbf{1}_{\{\tau_j\in\mathcal T\}} C(\theta_j)\Bigg], \end{aligned} }\end{equation} where $\tau^{(i) = \{\tau_j^{(i)}\}_{j=1 ^\infty$ (resp.}}
elements.formula-2411-009: \begin{equation}{  J^{(i)} (\tau^{(i)},\mathbf \theta^{(i)}) := \mathbb E\Bigg[\int_{\mathcal T} We^{-k^{(i)}t}p_t^{(i)}(l_t^{(i)}+r_t^c-r_t^{(i)}) dt - \sum_j 1_{\{\tau_j^{(i)}\in\mathcal T\}} C^{(i)}(\theta_j^{(i)})\Bigg], J(\mathbf \tau,\mathbf \theta) :=\mathbb E\Bigg[\int_{\mathcal T} We^{-kt}\bar p_t(l_t+r_t^c-r_t) dt - \sum_j 1_{\{\tau_j\in\mathcal T\}} C(\theta_j)\Bigg],  }\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-098: For simplicity, we now consider the case where there is one major bank and multiple minor banks.
elements.sent-2411-100: Initially, the state of the major player is fixed as $x_0^0$ and the distribution of the minor players is set to $\mu_0$, which is assumed to be supported on a compact set.
elements.sent-2411-101: At time $t\in [T]$, the states of the major bank are represented as $x_t^0 = (p_t^0,r_t^0)$, while for the representative minor bank, they are $x_t = (p_t,r_t)$.
elements.sent-2411-102: Along with the states, the central bank rate is denoted as $r_t^c$, and the mean-field flow $\mu_t$.
elements.sent-2411-103: In the discrete-time setting, impulse controls are treated similarly to regular controls.
elements.sent-2411-104: For any $t\in [T]$, the major and minor players will control $(u_t^0,u_t)$ to be their new interest rates, i.e., $r_{t+1 ^0 = u_t^0$ and $r_{t+1 = u_t$.}}
elements.sent-2411-105: Subsequently, the proportions transfer according to these new interest rates.
elements.sent-2411-106: Let $\mathcal X$ and $\mathcal U$ be the state and action space for minor players, $\mathcal X^0$ and $\mathcal U^0$ for the major player, and let $\mathcal R$ be the space of central bank rate.
elements.sent-2411-107: Let $K^0(x^0,u^0,\mu):\mathcal X^0\times \mathcal U^0\times \mathcal P(\mathcal X)\to \mathcal X^0$ and $K(x^0,u^0,x,u,\mu):\mathcal X^0\times \mathcal U^0\times \mathcal X\times \mathcal U\times \mathcal P(\mathcal X)\to \mathcal X$ be deterministic transition functions such that \begin{equation}{ \begin{aligned} K_p^0(x^0,u^0,\mu) = p^0 + b^0(x^0,u^0,\mu)\Delta t, \ \ K_r^0(x^0,u^0,\mu) = u^0, K_p(x^0,u^0,x,u,\mu) = x + b(x^0,u^0,x,u,\mu)\Delta t, \ \ K_r(x^0,u^0,x,u,\mu) = u, \end{aligned} }\end{equation} with $K^0 = (K^0_p,K^0_r)$ and $K = (K_p,K_r)$.
elements.formula-2411-010: \begin{equation}{  K_p^0(x^0,u^0,\mu) = p^0 + b^0(x^0,u^0,\mu)\Delta t, \ \ K_r^0(x^0,u^0,\mu) = u^0, K_p(x^0,u^0,x,u,\mu) = x + b(x^0,u^0,x,u,\mu)\Delta t, \ \ K_r(x^0,u^0,x,u,\mu) = u,  }\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-108: The drifts $b^0$ and $b$ are defined in \eqref{eq:density_transform_modified_integral_minor_rep and \eqref{eq:density_transform_modified_integral_major_MF for the case $M=1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-109: Let $P^c(r^c):\mathcal R\to P(\mathcal R)$ be the transition probability for the central bank rate.
elements.sent-2411-110: The states of players evolve as $x_{t+1 ^0 = K^0\left(x_t^0, u_t^0, \mu_t\right)$ for the major player and $x_{t+1 = P\left(x_t^0, u_t^0, x_t,u_t, \mu_t\right)$ for the minor players.}}
elements.sent-2411-111: The central bank rate transits as $r^c_{t+1 \sim P^c(r_t^c)$.}
elements.sent-2411-112: Let the control function of major player be $u_t^0(x^0,r^c,\mu):\mathcal X^0\times \mathcal R\times \mathcal P(\mathcal X)\to \mathcal U^0$, and for minor players $u_t(x^0,x,r^c,\mu):\mathcal X^0\times \mathcal X\times \mathcal R\times \mathcal P(\mathcal X)\to \mathcal U$.
elements.sent-2411-113: Given a sequential control functions $\mathbf u =( u_t)_{t\in\mathcal T}$, we define the operator \begin{equation}{ T_t^{\mathbf u}\left(x_t^0, u_t^0, r_t^c,\mu_t\right):=\int_{\mathcal X } K\Big(x_t^0, u_t^0,x, u_t(x_t^0,x, r_t^c, \mu_t), \mu\Big) \mu_t(d x) }\end{equation} to represent the mean field transition $\mu_t$ to $\mu_{t+1}$ , if all the minor plays apply the control function ${\mathbf u _t$ and the major player takes state $x_t^0$ and action $u_t^0$.}
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation}{ T_t^{\mathbf u}\left(x_t^0, u_t^0, r_t^c,\mu_t\right):=\int_{\mathcal X } K\Big(x_t^0, u_t^0,x, u_t(x_t^0,x, r_t^c, \mu_t), \mu\Big) \mu_t(d x) }\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-114: If we also define a sequence of major control functions as $\mathbf u^0 = (\mathbf u_t^0)_{t\in\mathcal T}$, the objective functions are defined as follows: \begin{equation}{ \begin{aligned} J^0\left(\mathbf u^0, \mathbf u\right)=\mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{t \in [T]} \gamma^tR_t^0\left(x_t^0, u_t^0(x_t^0,r_t^c, \mu_t), r_t^c,\mu_t\right)\right], J\left(\mathbf u^0, \mathbf u\right)=\mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{t \in [T]}\gamma^t R_t\left(x_t, u_t(x_t^0,x_t, r_t^c, \mu_t), r_t^c, \mu_t\right)\right], \end{aligned} }\end{equation} where $\gamma=\exp(-k^0\Delta t)=\exp(-k\Delta t)$ , and the running rewards are given by \begin{equation}{ \begin{aligned} R_t^0\left(x_t^0, u_t^0, r_t^c,\mu_t\right) = W p_{t}^0(l^0+r_t^c-u_t)-C^0(u_t^0-r_t^0), R_t\left(x_t, u_t, r_t^c, \mu_t\right) = Wp_{t}(l+r_t^c-u_t)-C(u_t-r_t). \end{aligned} }\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-012: \begin{equation}{  J^0\left(\mathbf u^0, \mathbf u\right)=E\left[\sum_{t \in [T]} \gamma^tR_t^0\left(x_t^0, u_t^0(x_t^0,r_t^c, \mu_t), r_t^c,\mu_t\right)\right], J\left(\mathbf u^0, \mathbf u\right)=E\left[\sum_{t \in [T]}\gamma^t R_t\left(x_t, u_t(x_t^0,x_t, r_t^c, \mu_t), r_t^c, \mu_t\right)\right],  }\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation}{  R_t^0\left(x_t^0, u_t^0, r_t^c,\mu_t\right) = W p_{t}^0(l^0+r_t^c-u_t)-C^0(u_t^0-r_t^0), R_t\left(x_t, u_t, r_t^c, \mu_t\right) = Wp_{t}(l+r_t^c-u_t)-C(u_t-r_t).  }\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-115: To define the Nash Equilibrium, we introduce an individual minor player who follows the control functions $\mathbf {\hat u}$, while other minor players continue to follow the controls $\mathbf u$.
elements.sent-2411-116: Note that this single minor player does not influence the evolution of other players.
elements.sent-2411-117: The objective function for this player is defined as: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} J\left(\mathbf u^0, \mathbf u,\mathbf {\hat u}\right)=\mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{t \in [T]} \gamma^t R_t\left(x_t^0, u_t^0, \hat x_t,\hat u_t ,\mu_t\right)\right]. \end{aligned}\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-014: \begin{equation} J\left(\mathbf u^0, \mathbf u,\mathbf {\hat u}\right)=E\left[\sum_{t \in [T]} \gamma^t R_t\left(x_t^0, u_t^0, \hat x_t,\hat u_t ,\mu_t\right)\right]. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-118: In our model, a control profile $(\mathbf u_*^0,\mathbf u_*)$ is called a Nash Equilibrium if for any alternative policies $\mathbf u^0$ and $\mathbf {\hat u}$, it satisfies the conditions \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} J^0\left(\mathbf u_*^0,\mathbf u_*\right) \geq J^0\left(\mathbf u^0,\mathbf u_*\right), J(\mathbf u_*^0,\mathbf u_*,\mathbf u_*) \geq J\left(\mathbf u_*^0,\mathbf u_*,\mathbf {\hat u}\right). \end{aligned}\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-015: \begin{equation} J^0\left(\mathbf u_*^0,\mathbf u_*\right) \geq J^0\left(\mathbf u^0,\mathbf u_*\right), J(\mathbf u_*^0,\mathbf u_*,\mathbf u_*) \geq J\left(\mathbf u_*^0,\mathbf u_*,\mathbf {\hat u}\right). \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-119: The Nash Equilibrium conditions indicate that the controls are optimal against those of other players.
elements.sent-2411-120: For the minor players, the controls are optimal even against other minor players.
elements.sent-2411-121: Therefore, according to Definition \ref{def:nash , we aim to solve a fixed point problem.}
elements.sent-2411-122: Now, we introduce our Deep Q-Network (DQN) algorithm, based on the parametrization of the action-value function (Q function).
elements.sent-2411-123: The algorithm aims to ensure that Bellman's equation holds or approximately holds.
elements.sent-2411-124: For example for the major player, the action value function, given the conjectured policies of the other players $\mathbf u$, should satisfy \begin{equation}\label{eq:Bellman_eq} { \begin{aligned} Q_{\mathbf u, \mathbf u^0}^0 (t, x^0, u^0, r^c, \mu)=\gamma^{t} R_t^0(x^0, u^0, r^c, \mu) + \max _{\tilde u^{0}\in\mathcal U^0} \int_{\mathcal R} P^c(d\tilde r^c| r^c) \cdot Q_{\mathbf u, \mathbf u^0}^0\big(t+1, K^0(x^0,u^0,\mu), \tilde u^{0}, \tilde r^c, T_t^{\mathbf u}(x^0, u^0, r^c, \mu)\big) . \end{aligned} }\end{equation} Note that this equation is equivalent to the one where \(\gamma\) appears before the integral, as commonly used in the reinforcement learning literature (see \cite{wiering2012reinforcement ).}
elements.formula-2411-016: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Bellman_eq} {  Q_{\mathbf u, \mathbf u^0}^0 (t, x^0, u^0, r^c, \mu)=\gamma^{t} R_t^0(x^0, u^0, r^c, \mu) + \max _{\tilde u^{0}\in\mathcal U^0} \int_{\mathcal R} P^c(d\tilde r^c| r^c) \cdot Q_{\mathbf u, \mathbf u^0}^0\big(t+1, K^0(x^0,u^0,\mu), \tilde u^{0}, \tilde r^c, T_t^{\mathbf u}(x^0, u^0, r^c, \mu)\big) .  }\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-125: We can consider a discounted Q function to obtain this formulation.
elements.sent-2411-126: To parametrize the Q function by a neural network, we approximate the measure $\mu\in\mathcal P(\mathcal X)$ using finite-dimensional vectors.
elements.sent-2411-127: It is necessary since the measure object lies in an infinite dimensional space and we should consider the entire measure as an input.
elements.sent-2411-128: This process is called the projection of a mean-field measure.
elements.sent-2411-129: It is feasible given the assumption that $\mu_0$ is compactly supported, we can estimate a compact subset $\mathcal X^c$ such that $\text{supp (\mu_t)\subset \mathcal X^c$ for all $t\in \mathcal T$, since both $b^0$ and $b$ are bounded.}
elements.sent-2411-130: Let $\mathcal X^F = (x^{(i) )_{i=1 ^{N_\epsilon}$ be a $\epsilon-$net for the compact set $\mathcal X^c$.}}
elements.sent-2411-131: Elements of $\mu\in \mathcal P(\mathcal X^F)$ can be expressed as vectors $N_\epsilon$-length of length $N_\epsilon$.
elements.sent-2411-132: We define the projection operator $\mathcal A:\mathcal P(\mathcal X^c)\to \mathcal P(\mathcal X^F)$ such that $\mathcal A(\mu_t)\in \mathcal P(\mathcal X^F)$ is a good approximation of $\mu_t\in \mathcal P(\mathcal X^c)$.
elements.sent-2411-133: Then, we modify Bellman's equation \eqref{eq:Bellman_eq as follows \begin{equation}\label{eq:Bellman_eq_modified} { \begin{aligned} \hat Q_{\mathbf u, \mathbf u^0}^0(t, x^0, u^0, r^c, \mu)=\hat Q_{\mathbf u, \mathbf u^0}^0(t, x^0, u^0, r^c, \mathcal A(\mu)) =\gamma^t R_t^0(x^0, u^0, r^c, \mathcal A(\mu))+\max _{\tilde u^{0}\in \mathcal U^0}\int_{\mathcal R} P^c(d\tilde r^c| r^c) \cdot \hat Q_{\mathbf u, \mathbf u^0}^0\Big(t+1, K^0(x^0,u^0,\mathcal A(\mu)), \tilde u^{0}, \tilde r^c,\mathcal A\big(T_t^{\mathbf u}(x^0, u^0, \tilde r^c, \mathcal A(\mu))\big)\Big). \end{aligned} }\end{equation} Based on the approximated Bellman's equation \eqref{eq:Bellman_eq_modified , we represent $\hat Q_{\mathbf u, \mathbf u^0 ^0(t, x^0, u^0, r^c, \mathcal A(\mu))$ as a neural network in the form $\hat Q(\theta;\omega)$, where the inputs are $\theta^0 = \big(t,x^0,u^0,r^c,(\mu_t^{(i) )_{i=1 ^{N_\epsilon}\big)$, with $\sum_{i=1 ^{N_\epsilon \mu_t^{(i) = 1$ and $\mu_t^{(i)}$ representing the mass at point $x_t^i$.}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-017: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Bellman_eq_modified} {  \hat Q_{\mathbf u, \mathbf u^0}^0(t, x^0, u^0, r^c, \mu)=\hat Q_{\mathbf u, \mathbf u^0}^0(t, x^0, u^0, r^c, \mathcal A(\mu)) =\gamma^t R_t^0(x^0, u^0, r^c, \mathcal A(\mu))+\max _{\tilde u^{0}\in \mathcal U^0}\int_{\mathcal R} P^c(d\tilde r^c| r^c) \cdot \hat Q_{\mathbf u, \mathbf u^0}^0\Big(t+1, K^0(x^0,u^0,\mathcal A(\mu)), \tilde u^{0}, \tilde r^c,\mathcal A\big(T_t^{\mathbf u}(x^0, u^0, \tilde r^c, \mathcal A(\mu))\big)\Big).  }\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-134: We use optimization algorithms for neural networks to update the load parameters $\omega$.
elements.sent-2411-135: Similarly, we derive the approximated equality for the Q-function of the representative minor player as \begin{equation}\label{eq:Bellman_eq_modified_minor} { \begin{aligned} \hat Q_{\mathbf u, \mathbf u^0}(t, x^0, x,u,r^c, \mu) =\hat Q_{\mathbf u, \mathbf u^0}(t, x^0,x,u, r^c, \mathcal A(\mu)) =\gamma^t R_t(x, u, r^c, \mathcal A(\mu))+\max _{\tilde u\in \mathcal U}\int_{\mathcal R} P^c(d\tilde r^c| r^c) \cdot \hat Q_{\mathbf u, \mathbf u^0}\Big(t+1, K^0(\cdot), K(\cdot), \tilde u, \tilde r^c,\mathcal A\big(T_t^{\mathbf u}(x^0, \mathbf u^0, r^c, \mathcal A(\mu))\big)\Big) \end{aligned} }\end{equation} where { $K^0(\cdot) \coloneq K^0(x^0,\mathbf u^0,\mathcal A(\mu))$} and { $K(\cdot) \coloneq K(x^0,u^0,x, u,\mathcal A(\mu))$}.
elements.formula-2411-018: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Bellman_eq_modified_minor} {  \hat Q_{\mathbf u, \mathbf u^0}(t, x^0, x,u,r^c, \mu) =\hat Q_{\mathbf u, \mathbf u^0}(t, x^0,x,u, r^c, \mathcal A(\mu)) =\gamma^t R_t(x, u, r^c, \mathcal A(\mu))+\max _{\tilde u\in \mathcal U}\int_{\mathcal R} P^c(d\tilde r^c| r^c) \cdot \hat Q_{\mathbf u, \mathbf u^0}\Big(t+1, K^0(\cdot), K(\cdot), \tilde u, \tilde r^c,\mathcal A\big(T_t^{\mathbf u}(x^0, \mathbf u^0, r^c, \mathcal A(\mu))\big)\Big)  }\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-136: We use the function $\mathcal S \hat Q^0(\cdot)$ and $\mathcal S\hat Q(\cdot)$ to denote the right-hand side of the equations \eqref{eq:Bellman_eq_modified and \eqref{eq:Bellman_eq_modified_minor , respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-137: In fact, in training, we are only concerned with projecting probability measure supported on finite points to $\mathcal P (\mathcal X^F)$.
elements.sent-2411-138: Denote the original probability measure $\mu$ with supports $(p_k,r_k)_{k\in [K]}$ where $\mu_k = \mu((p_k,r_k))$ and $\sum_{k\in[K] \mu_k = 1$.}
elements.sent-2411-139: For each $k\in [K]$, the corresponding support $(p_k,r_k)$ falls into a sub-rectangle, i.e., $(p_k,r_k)\in (p_{(i) ,p_{(i+1) )\times (r_{(j) ,r_{(j+1) )$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-140: We define a new measure $\tilde \mu^k$ supported on the vertices of this sub-rectangle (see Figure \ref{fig:sample_projection ) as \begin{equation}\tilde \mu_k(p,r) = \left\{\begin{array}{ll} (p_{(i+1)}-p_k)(r_{(j+1)}-r_k)\mu_k, \text{if}\ (p,r)=(p_{(i)},r_{(j)}); (p_{(i+1)}-p_k)(r_k - r_{(j)})\mu_k, \text{if}\ (p,r)=(p_{(i)},r_{(j+1)}); (p_k-p_{(i)})(r_{(j+1)}-r_k)\mu_k, \text{if}\ (p,r)=(p_{(i+1)},r_{(j)}); (p_k-p_{(i)})(r_k - r_{(j)})\mu_k, \text{if}\ (p,r)=(p_{(i+1)},r_{(j+1)}); 0, \text{otherwise}. \end{array}\right.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-141: As an approximation of the original finite supported distribution $\mu$, we define $\mathcal A(\mu) = \sum_{k\in [K] \tilde \mu_k\in\mathcal P(\mathcal X^F)$.}
elements.paragraph-2411-007: Fictitious Play and Average Neural Networks
elements.sent-2411-142: To stabilize the convergence process, we use a moving average of the past Q functions.
elements.sent-2411-143: Denoting $Q_n^0$ and $Q_n$ as the approximated Q-functions at the $n$-th iteration, we derive the optimal control functions $u_n^0$ and $u_n$ from these Q functions.
elements.sent-2411-144: We then evaluate the Q-functions according to the Bellman Equations \eqref{eq:Bellman_eq_modified and \eqref{eq:Bellman_eq_modified_minor and the strategies of the opposite players.}}
elements.sent-2411-145: Let $\hat Q_{n ^0$, $\hat Q_{n}$ represent the evaluated Q functions.}
elements.sent-2411-146: We update the Q-function as $Q_{n+1 \leftarrow \frac{n {n+1 Q_{n + \frac{1 {n+1 \hat Q_{n $, $Q_{n+1 ^0 \leftarrow \frac{n {n+1 Q_{n ^0 + \frac{1 {n+1 \hat Q_{n ^0 $ and pass to the next step (see Algorithm \ref{alg:DQN ).}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-147: The challenge lies in averaging neural networks due to their non-linear nature, as mentioned in \cite{lauriere2022scalable .}
elements.sent-2411-148: In our experiments, we use fully connected neural networks with one hidden layer as our architecture.
elements.sent-2411-149: The parameters of neural networks can be interpreted as measures, as described in \cite{chen2023entropic, hu2021mean .}
elements.sent-2411-150: It can be represented as \begin{equation}\label{eq:measure_landscape_intro} \frac{1}{L} \sum_{l=1}^L \beta_{l} \varphi\left(\alpha_{ l} \cdot \theta+\gamma_{l}\right)=\int_{\mathbb{R}^{d+2}} \beta \varphi(\alpha \cdot \theta+\gamma) m^L(d \beta d \alpha d \gamma)\end{equation} where we denote the input $z\in\mathbb R^d$, parameters as multiplier $\alpha$, bias $\gamma$ and weight $\beta$, and $m^L=\frac{1 {L \sum_{l=1 ^L \delta_{\left(\beta_{l , \alpha_{l , \gamma_{l}\right) .$ For simplicity, denote $x=(\beta, \alpha, \gamma) \in \mathbb{R ^{d+2}$ and $\hat{\varphi (x, z)=\beta \varphi(\alpha \cdot z+\gamma)$, so the neural network output can be represented as $\hat Q(\theta;m)= \mathbb{E ^{X\sim m [\hat{\varphi (X, z)]$.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-151: Thus, when the width $L$ becomes large, we can understand the load parameters as a measure.
elements.sent-2411-152: This allows us to transfer the problem from averaging the outputs of neural networks to averaging the measures, which is more feasible in implementation.
elements.sent-2411-153: To be precise, we parameterized $\hat Q(\theta;m_{n ) = \mathbb{E ^{X\sim m_n [\hat{\varphi (X, \theta)]$, $\hat Q(\theta;\tilde m_n) = \mathbb{E ^{X\sim \tilde m_{n [\hat{\varphi (X, \theta)]$, the update of the moving average can be expressed as \begin{equation}\label{eq:ficititous_play_process} \begin{aligned} \hat Q(z;m_{n+1}) = \frac{n}{n+1}\hat Q(z;m_{n}) + \frac{1}{n+1} \hat Q(z;\tilde m_n) \quad \quad \Leftrightarrow m_{n+1} = \frac{n}{n+1} m_{n} + \frac{1}{n+1} \tilde m_{n}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} The weighted average of the measures can be achieved by combining neurons through random selection.}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-021: \begin{equation}\label{eq:ficititous_play_process}  \hat Q(z;m_{n+1}) = \frac{n}{n+1}\hat Q(z;m_{n}) + \frac{1}{n+1} \hat Q(z;\tilde m_n) \quad \quad \Leftrightarrow m_{n+1} = \frac{n}{n+1} m_{n} + \frac{1}{n+1} \tilde m_{n}. \end{equation}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Deep Q-Network (DQN) Algorithm} \label{alg:DQN} \KwIn{Approximate $\epsilon$-net $\mathcal X^F$, transition functions $K,K^0$ and $P^c$, drift function $b^0,b$, the reward function $R^0,R$, iteration number of outer iteration $N$, iteration number of inner iteration for network update $M$, batch size $B$;} Initialize network $\hat Q^0(\theta^0;\omega_{0,0}^0)$ and $\hat Q(\theta;\omega_{0,0})$; \For{$n=0,1,..N-1$}{ Set $u_t^0(x^0,r^c,\mu) = \text{argmax}_{u^0\in \mathcal U^0} \hat Q^0(t,x^0,u^0,r^c,\mu;\omega_{n,0}^0)$, $u_t(x^0,x,r^c,\mu) = \text{argmax}_{u\in \mathcal U} \hat Q^0(t,x^0,x,u,r^c,\mu;\omega_{n,0})$ for $t\in\mathcal T$, $x\in\mathcal X$, $x^0\in \mathcal X^0$, $r^c\in \mathcal R$, $\mu\in \mathcal P(\mathcal X^F)$ ; \For{$m=0,1,..M-1$}{ Sample a batch of inputs as $\big\{(t_i,x^0_i,u_i^0,x_i,u_i,r^c_i,(\mu^{(j)}_i)_{j=1}^{N_\epsilon}\big\}_{i=1}^{B}$; Update $\omega_{n,m}^0\mapsto \omega_{n,m+1}^0$ by optimizers based on loss: \begin{equation}L^0(\omega^0) = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{i=1}^{B}\big|\hat Q^0(\theta_i^0;\omega^0) - \mathcal S \hat Q^0(\theta_i^0;\omega^0) \big|^2;\end{equation} Update $\omega_{n,m}\mapsto \omega_{n, m+1}$ by optimizers based on loss: \begin{equation}L(\omega) = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{i=1}^{B}\big|\hat Q(\theta_i;\omega) - \mathcal S \hat Q(\theta_i;\omega) \big|^2;\end{equation}} Update $Q^0,Q$ in a fictitious play way, as in \eqref{eq:ficititous_play_process}: \begin{equation} \hat Q^0(\theta^0;\omega_{n+1,0}^0) = \frac{n}{n+1} \hat Q^0(\theta^0;\omega_{n,0}^0) + \frac{1}{n+1}\hat Q^0(\theta^0; \omega_{n,M}^0); \hat Q(\theta;\omega_{n+1,0}) = \frac{n}{n+1}\hat Q(\theta;\omega_{n,0}) + \frac{1}{n+1}\hat Q(\theta; \omega_{n,M}) ; \end{equation} } Define the control functions: \begin{equation*} \begin{aligned} u_t^0(x^0,r^c,\mu) = u_t^0(x^0,r^c,\mathcal A(\mu)) = \quad \quad \quad \quad\quad \quad\text{argmax}_{u^0\in \mathcal U^0} \hat Q^0(t,x^0,u^0,r^c,\mu;\omega_{N,0}^0), u_t(x^0,x,R,\mu) = u_t(x^0,x,r^c,\mathcal A(\mu)) = \quad \quad \quad \quad\quad \quad\text{argmax}_{u\in \mathcal U} \hat Q^0(t,x^0,x,u,r^c,\mu;\omega_{N,0}), \end{aligned} \end{equation*} for any $t\in\mathcal T$, $x\in\mathcal X$, $x^0\in \mathcal X^0$, $r^c\in \mathcal R$, $\mu\in \mathcal P(\mathcal X)$; \KwOut{The control functions $u_t^0$ and $u_t$.}
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{minipage}{0.48\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth,height=4cm]{figures/bellman_loss.pdf} \captionof{figure}{Average losses of 10 individual trainings (shadowed) and its \(200\) rolling average (bold).} \label{fig:error-gap} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.48\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth,height=4cm]{figures/dynamic_evolution.pdf} \captionof{figure}{The evolution of major player's state and central bank rate. } \label{fig:dynamic_evolution} \end{minipage}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/mean_field_evolution.pdf} \caption{The evolution of mean field measure (minor players).} \label{fig:mean_field_evolution}
elements.sent-2411-154: In our experiment, we assume the time horizon of the banks is $T=5$, with a decision period $\Delta t =1$.
elements.sent-2411-155: For clients' preference, the escape rate and viscosity parameter of banks, we set $(\kappa^0, \kappa)=(5,5)$ and $(\delta^0,\delta)=(0.1\%,0.1\%)$.
elements.sent-2411-156: The permissible interest rate selection range $\mathcal U^0 = \mathcal U = [2.5\%,3.5\%]$, the state space $\mathcal X^0 = \mathcal X = [20\%,80\%] \times [2.5\%,3.5\%]$.
elements.sent-2411-157: Given these parameters and the definition of $b^0,b$ (formulated in \eqref{eq:density_transform_modified_integral_minor_rep and \eqref{eq:density_transform_modified_integral_major_MF ), we have \begin{equation}\label{eq:b_bound} |b^0|,|b|\le \overline \kappa (r_{max}-r_{min} - \delta) = 4.5\%.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-158: This implies the proportion of both major and minor players will change by at most $2\%$ due to the interest rate competition.
elements.sent-2411-159: Initially, for the state of the major player, we set $\bar p^0=50\%$, $r^0=3\%$, while for minor players we set their distribution to be uniform $\mu_0=U([40\%.60\%]\times [2.5\%,3.5\%])$.
elements.sent-2411-160: Given the $b^0$, $b$ bound \eqref{eq:b_bound , we deduce that the compact support of $\mu_t$ will not escape from $[20\%.80\%]\times [2.5\%,3.5\%]$, i.e. $\mathcal X^c = \mathcal X$.}
elements.sent-2411-161: Then, we evenly divide the space $\mathcal X^c$ into $12\times 5$ sub-rectangles.
elements.sent-2411-163: Throughout our paper, we set the discounting $\gamma = 0.9$ and the total deposit volume $W=1$ without loss of generality.
elements.sent-2411-164: The liquidity premiums are set to $l^0=0$, $l=0.1\%$.
elements.sent-2411-165: Regarding the cost incurred from interest rate changes, we set the cost function $C^0(\Delta r) = C(\Delta r) = 10\% \cdot |\Delta r| +0.1\% \cdot \mathbf 1_{\Delta r\neq 0 $.}
elements.sent-2411-166: The set of all central bank rates is $\mathcal R = (r^{c,i )_{i=1 ^3 = [2.5\%,3\%,3.5\%]$, the transition function of the central bank rate $r^c$ is given by $P^c(r_{t+\Delta t ^c=r^{c,i' |r_t^{c =r^{c,i )=\lambda \Delta t \mathbf{1 _{i=i' + (1-\lambda \Delta t)\mathbf{1 _{i\neq i' $ with $\lambda = 0.2$, and initially we fix $r^c_i = 3\%$.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-167: For the neural network architecture, we deploy a fully connected network with one hidden layer and a width of $L=256$.
elements.sent-2411-168: We set the outer iteration number to $N=100$, the inner iteration number to $M=400$, and the batch size to $B = 240$.
elements.sent-2411-169: The Adam algorithm is used for the training, with learning rates set to $0.001$.
elements.sent-2411-170: In our experiment, the training process takes 12 minutes to complete on the CPU Intel Core i7-9750H.
elements.sent-2411-171: Under our parameter settings, we observed a decrease in the Bellman loss on batch, as shown in Figure \ref{fig:error-gap .}
elements.sent-2411-172: The Bellman loss for both the major player's Q function and the minor players' functions alternates in descent but generally exhibits a decreasing trend.
elements.sent-2411-173: After about half of the training, the average loss remains below \(1 \times 10^{-7}\).
elements.sent-2411-174: We also observe from Figure \ref{fig:dynamic_evolution and \ref{fig:mean_field_evolution that all banks consistently set lower deposit interest rates, regardless of fluctuations in the central bank's policy rate.}}

elements.sent-2411-182: Our algorithm has demonstrated stability in convergence and robustness in repeated tests, Bellman losses between iteration times $10,000$ and $20,000$, its maximum standard error over 10 tests is $7.23\times 10^{-7}$ (major player) and $2.48\times 10^{-7}$ (minor player) after $10000$ iterations.
elements.sent-2411-183: In this paper, we model the bank interest rate decision problem as an impulsive major-minor mean field game against other players in the market.
elements.sent-2411-184: Banks adjust their interest rates to compete for market share, taking into account client attraction and deposit income.
elements.sent-2411-185: The banks are not symmetric, with finite major banks and multiple minor banks operating simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-186: We developed a DQN algorithm to solve this major-minor mean field game, introducing neural network approximation and averaging of networks.
elements.sent-2411-187: Our results demonstrate an approximated decision process of players under the Nash Equilibrium, providing insights into banks' decisions in adjusting interest rates to cope with competition.. \bibliographystyle{ACM-Reference-Format.}
elements.sent-2411-188: In our experiment, we assume that the time horizon of the banks is $T=5$, with a decision period $\Delta t =1$.

elements.sent-2411-201: In this paper, we model the bank interest rate decision problem as an impulsive major-minor mean-field game against other players in the market.
elements.sent-2411-202: We developed a DQN algorithm to solve this major-minor mean-field game, introducing neural network approximation and averaging of networks.
elements.sent-2411-203: Our results demonstrate an approximated decision process of players under the Nash Equilibrium, providing insights into banks' decisions in adjusting interest rates to cope with competition.\footnote{Disclaimer.}
